contraventionDescription: Parked in a restricted street during prescribed hours. You were parked on a yellow line (single or double) or in a street where there are temporary waiting restrictions

sections.heading: How to have your penalty charge notice (PCN) for this parking contravention cancelled.
sections.content: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.
sections.contentHtml: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.

sections.heading: Follow our 3 stage process:
sections.content:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.
2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.
3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.
sections.contentHtml:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.

2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.

3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.

sections.heading: Signs and lines
sections.content:
This section is very important. We can’t emphasise enough how often the signage is incorrect or indeed there is no signage whatsoever. Road markings can conflict with the signs and quite often the road markings are not clearly visible. If the signs or lines are wrong then you will have grounds to have your parking ticket (penalty charge notice) cancelled.
The rules on signage and lineage on yellow lines are actually quite simple.
Double yellow lines –The double yellow lines must be clear and have a “T” bar at each end so a motorist can clearly see where they start and end. Unless otherwise indicated by signs double yellow lines mean no waiting at any time. There is no longer any requirement for an enforcing authority to place yellow “at any time” signs adjacent to double yellow lines. Some double lines do have signs where for example they only apply at certain times of the year, for example in seaside resorts “30th May – 30th September”
Single yellow lines –The lines must be clear and have “T” bars at the end showing where they start and end.  Also were other road markings visible at the point where you parked for example, were there markings from a previous parking bay visible? Or were the number of road markings confusing? Obviously if there were other markings visible for example a previous parking bay then you should appeal.
There must be clear roadside signage at the point of parking showing the hours that they are operational (enforced). These signs must have the “no waiting” symbol and the times and days of enforcement clearly shown. (Please note if the signs do not specify dates then the restriction apply on every day) They must be yellow (not white) and the writing in black. They cannot be enforced 24 hours as then double yellow lines would be required. The signs should be at maximum intervals of 30 metres and on every section of yellow line. If there is a break in the yellow line such as for a parking bay or a driveway then there must be signs on each section of the line.
However there is a dispensation for local authorities not to put signs on single yellow lines where they are located within a compliant controlled parking zone. (CPZ)

Road markings can conflict with the signs and quite often the road markings are not clearly visible. If the signs or lines are wrong then you will have grounds to have your parking ticket (penalty charge notice) cancelled.
The rules on signage and lineage on yellow lines are actually quite simple.

Double yellow lines –The double yellow lines must be clear and have a “T” bar at each end so a motorist can clearly see where they start and end. Unless otherwise indicated by signs double yellow lines mean no waiting at any time. There is no longer any requirement for an enforcing authority to place yellow “at any time” signs adjacent to double yellow lines. Some double lines do have signs where for example they only apply at certain times of the year, for example in seaside resorts “30th May – 30th September”

Single yellow lines –The lines must be clear and have “T” bars at the end showing where they start and end.  Also were other road markings visible at the point where you parked for example, were there markings from a previous parking bay visible? Or were the number of road markings confusing? Obviously if there were other markings visible for example a previous parking bay then you should appeal.

There must be clear roadside signage at the point of parking showing the hours that they are operational (enforced). They cannot be enforced 24 hours as then double yellow lines would be required. The signs should be at maximum intervals of 30 metres and on every section of yellow line. However there is a dispensation for local authorities not to put signs on single yellow lines where they are located within a compliant controlled parking zone. (CPZ)

sections.heading: Controlled parking zones
sections.content:
You can obtain maps showing the borders of a controlled parking zone from the enforcing authority and often from their websites.
A Controlled Parking Zone (CPZ) is a designated area (usually a town centre) which has the same enforcement hours on single yellow lines and normally chargeable hours on pay and display bays within the zone. If the hours of enforcement are different on some single yellow lines to the CPZ times then there must be clear roadside signage showing this. However there are strict signage requirements and CPZ signs must be erected at all entry points to the zone and a zone itself should not really be larger than 12 streets. See statutory guidance. Very often the signs are non compliant. If you can’t find a CPZ entry sign then usually the pay and display periods are the same as the operational hours of the single yellow lines.
Below we show diagrams of the signs and lines which must be in situ by law and much more information that will help you decide whether to appeal against your penalty charge notice.
If you are in a Controlled Parking Zone (CPZ), on entering the zone, were the correct signs located on  at each entry point? (Quite often they are not) They should not be located close to a junction where your attention could be elsewhere. Were they facing the right direction?  Were they clearly visible from your car. (ie. Not obstructed, defaced etc). If not, unless there is clear road-side signage indicating parking restrictions on each section of single yellow line as stated above and within a maximum of 15 metres (3 car lengths) of where you actually parked on the same stretch of yellow line, your PCN should be cancelled as the contravention did not occur. Local authorities must use correct signage for their controlled parking zones otherwise the controlled parking zone is unenforceable (except sections of the roads where there is clear road side signage) London authorities do not sign post their controlled parking zones correctly and have been  ruled “unlawful” by a parking adjudicator. See Parking adjudicators decisions.
Also in London there are many Red routes. These are not enforced by the local councils rather by transport for London. Interestingly therefore a controlled parking zone cannot have a red route within it. There must be separate zones each side of a red route and therefore controlled zone signs on every road off of every red route. See Red route enforcement.
 

Above: Controlled parking zone signs that must be placed at each entry point to a controlled parking zone. Please also remember that a controlled parking zone should not be more than about a dozen streets in total as stated in the statutory guidance. At the end of each controlled parking zone the “Zone ends” signs should be erected. Without this how would you know that you have or indeed have not left the zone area. Westminster council in London does not erect the zone end signs making it even more difficult to work out when the restrictions on single yellow lines and pay and display bays apply.
It is important to note the information in the boxes immediately underneath each picture as this shows the circumstance in which the sign can be used and also the permitted variations to it. If the sign where you parked is different to the one below and is not a permitted variant your parking ticket could be cancelled.
 

Above: This sign is usually used on double yellow lines in tourist areas such as the seaside when double yellow lines only apply for part of each day or year.
 

Above: This sign is for use on single yellow lines and must be placed at intervals of no more that 15 metres and on every stretch of single yellow line unless the line is in a properly marked and valid controlled parking zone. Andy this is the sign number 639. the other loading signs are not needed for this section.
Go to top
 
Lines
Road Markings must be present, clear, unobstructed by leaves etc and of the correct width in order for any restrictions to be enforceable.
Please note the information regarding sizes and the fact that no variants are permitted.
 

 

Go to top
 
Loading
You can park on a single yellow line if you are loading and if loading is not prohibited. (please see the contravention code 2 loading section)
Some local authorities allow a grace period on a single yellow line. You should check with them to make sure that you were allowed the grace period.
Please don’t forget that you can cite compassionate grounds in any appeal.
Adjudicator’s decisions. There have been many adjudicators’ decisions on loading. See adjudicators decisions on loading/unloading.
Back to Parking

Andy this is the sign number 639. the other loading signs are not needed for this section.

Lines

Road Markings must be present, clear, unobstructed by leaves etc and of the correct width in order for any restrictions to be enforceable.

Please note the information regarding sizes and the fact that no variants are permitted.

 

 

Go to top

 

Loading

You can park on a single yellow line if you are loading and if loading is not prohibited. (please see the contravention code 2 loading section)

Some local authorities allow a grace period on a single yellow line. You should check with them to make sure that you were allowed the grace period.

Please don’t forget that you can cite compassionate grounds in any appeal.

Adjudicator’s decisions. There have been many adjudicators’ decisions on loading. See adjudicators decisions on loading/unloading.

Back to Parking
